prompt_md: In a multi-file C++ program, `int score { 0 };` is defined in `game.cpp`. Another file, `display.cpp`, needs to read `score`. What should `display.cpp` contain?

options.a: `int score { 0 };` ‚Äî redefine it with the same initial value.
options.b: `extern int score;` ‚Äî a forward declaration that the linker resolves.
options.c: `static int score;` ‚Äî mark it static so the linker finds it.
options.d: Nothing ‚Äî global variables are automatically visible in all files.

explanation_md: `extern int score;` is a forward declaration telling the compiler that `score` is defined elsewhere with external linkage. The linker connects the declaration in `display.cpp` to the definition in `game.cpp`. Redefining the variable (option a) violates the One Definition Rule and causes a linker error. `static` makes a name *less* visible, not more.

prompt_md: What is the linkage of `const int limit { 50 };` defined at file scope in a `.cpp` file, with no other keywords?

options.a: External ‚Äî `const` globals are visible to all translation units.
options.b: Internal ‚Äî `const` globals default to internal linkage in C++.
options.c: No linkage ‚Äî `const` makes the variable local to the block.
options.d: External ‚Äî all global variables have external linkage by default.

explanation_md: `const` (and `constexpr`) global variables default to internal linkage in C++. Only non-`const` global variables default to external linkage. To give a `const` global external linkage you must explicitly add `extern` to the definition.

`value` is defined in `unit1.cpp` with external linkage (non-`const` global, no `static`). `unit2.cpp` forward-declares it with `extern int value;`. `doubleIt()` modifies the single shared `value`, setting it to `7 * 2 = 14`. `main` then prints that shared value.

prompt_md: A developer writes `extern int x { 0 };` in one `.cpp` file and `extern int x;` in another. Is this valid?

options.a: No ‚Äî `extern` and an initializer cannot appear together.
options.b: Yes ‚Äî `extern int x { 0 };` is the definition; `extern int x;` is the declaration.
options.c: Yes ‚Äî both lines are declarations; the linker synthesizes a definition automatically.
options.d: No ‚Äî `extern int x;` without an initializer is a compile error.

explanation_md: In C++, `extern` combined with an initializer (`extern int x { 0 };`) is still a definition ‚Äî it allocates storage. `extern` without an initializer (`extern int x;`) is a pure declaration. So the two lines together correctly express one definition and one forward declaration.

options.a: `bumpCounter()` is defined in `file_a.cpp` but not declared `extern` in `file_b.cpp`.
options.b: `int counter { 0 };` in `file_b.cpp` is a second definition, not a forward declaration; it should be `extern int counter;`.
options.c: `counter` in `file_a.cpp` needs `static` to make it externally visible.
options.d: The `void bumpCounter();` forward declaration in `file_b.cpp` is missing `extern`.

explanation_md: `int counter { 0 };` in `file_b.cpp` is a full definition (with initialization), creating a second `counter` ‚Äî a One Definition Rule violation. The fix is `extern int counter;` (no initializer), which is a declaration only. The linker then resolves both files' uses to the single definition in `file_a.cpp`.
